Part B.key_evidence: Science shows a bright extended source (galaxy) with a point-like candidate at center. Reference shows the same galaxy. Difference image appears very noisy/striped but with a central positive residual. Metadata: nearest PS1 source is 5.38 arcsec away with sgscore1=0.06 (galaxy-like), PS1 colors (g-r~0.78) consistent with a galaxy. ndethist=12 over deltajd=37.9 days indicates repeated detections at this position over ~a month. Positive subtraction in r band at magpsf=19.6.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with a supernova: a new point-like positive residual embedded in/near an extended galaxy host (sgscore1=0.06, nearby PS1 galaxy at 5.4"), showing sustained detections over ~38 days—typical SN timescale. The positive subtraction and brightness (~19.6 mag) fit a SN on a host.
Part B.alternative_analysis: An AGN is possible since the candidate could be near the galaxy nucleus and AGN can produce repeated positive detections; however, the 5.4" offset from the PS1 source and finite ~38-day detection span without clearly stochastic long-term behavior favor SN. A noisy difference image also raises some concern of subtraction artifacts, but the Science/Reference clearly show a real host galaxy.
